conceptual_framework.prompt: Management is deciding whether an omitted contingency disclosure would be material because it could change a lender's decision about renewing a credit line.
conceptual_framework.source: FASB Concepts Statement No. 8, Chapter 3 (materiality is an entity-specific aspect of relevance)

conceptual_framework.prompt: An estimate includes all necessary descriptions and explanations, is not biased toward a desired outcome, and is based on the best information available at issuance.
A segment revenue measure is useful because it lets analysts compare actual current margins with the margins they forecasted from prior-period information.
A warranty liability estimate is described transparently, includes the full population of claims, and avoids intentionally optimistic assumptions.

tax_timing.prompt: A company uses straight-line depreciation for financial reporting and accelerated depreciation for tax reporting. The asset's tax basis and book basis will reverse as future depreciation deductions differ.
tax_timing.source: ASC 740-10-25-20 (temporary differences arise from basis differences that result in taxable or deductible amounts in future years)

tax_timing.prompt: A corporation earns municipal bond interest that is included in pretax accounting income but excluded from taxable income and will never be taxed in a future period.
tax_timing.source: ASC 740-10-25-3 (income taxes are based on taxable income; permanent items do not reverse)

tax_timing.prompt: A company accrues warranty expense for book purposes when products are sold but deducts warranty costs on its tax return only when repairs are actually paid.
A company pays nondeductible penalties for violating environmental regulations. The expense reduces book income, but the tax law never allows a deduction for the penalty.
A seller recognizes revenue currently for book purposes but defers taxable income under an installment-sale method until cash collections occur.
A corporation receives life insurance proceeds on a key-person policy. The proceeds are included in book income but are excluded from taxable income and will not reverse later.

pensions_equity.prompt: During the year, employees provide services that earn additional pension benefits under the plan formula. The actuary measures the present value of benefits attributed to that current-year service.
pensions_equity.source: ASC 715-30-35-4 (service cost is the actuarial present value of benefits attributed to employee service during the period)

pensions_equity.prompt: At the start of the year, a defined benefit plan has an existing projected benefit obligation. The obligation increases during the year because settlement is one year closer and the discount rate unwinds.
pensions_equity.source: ASC 715-30-35-8 (interest cost is interest on the projected benefit obligation)

pensions_equity.prompt: A company amends its pension estimate for benefits earned by employees working in the current period, before considering expected return on plan assets or amortization items.
A pension obligation of $10 million exists at the beginning of the year. Using the settlement discount rate, the actuary computes the cost caused by the passage of time on that beginning obligation.
Employees worked another year under a defined benefit plan, increasing the benefits they have earned for future retirement. The cost is attributed to the services rendered this year.
A projected benefit obligation carried from the prior year grows because payment dates are closer, even before considering new benefits earned by current service.

government_nfp.prompt: A city prepares its statement of net position, reporting capital assets and long-term bonds payable using the economic resources measurement focus and accrual basis.
government_nfp.source: GASB 34 (government-wide financial statements use economic resources measurement focus and accrual basis)

government_nfp.prompt: A city prepares the general fund balance sheet, focusing on current financial resources and recording expenditures rather than depreciation on capital assets.
government_nfp.source: GASB 34 (governmental fund statements use current financial resources measurement focus and modified accrual basis)

government_nfp.prompt: A county reports infrastructure assets and accumulated depreciation in a statement that aggregates governmental activities as a whole.
A special revenue fund recognizes property tax revenue only when it is measurable and available to finance current-period expenditures.
government_nfp.source: GASB 33 and GASB 34 (modified accrual availability criterion for governmental funds)

government_nfp.prompt: A city records depreciation expense on police vehicles and reports the related capital assets in the governmental activities column.
A capital projects fund reports spending on a new fire station as an expenditure and focuses on spendable financial resources available for the project.

tbs.prompt: Ridge Co. acquires a new packaging machine. The vendor invoice is $200,000, against which Ridge takes a 2% cash discount. Ridge also pays freight-in of $5,000, installation of $8,000, and pre-production testing of $2,000. Separately, Ridge pays $3,000 for the machine's first-year insurance and $1,500 to train operators. Determine the amounts below. Round to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: Cost classification
exhibits.body: Capitalize only costs necessary to bring the asset to its condition and location for intended use: invoice net of the 2% discount ($196,000) + freight-in ($5,000) + installation ($8,000) + testing ($2,000). First-year insurance and operator training are period costs (expensed).

steps.label: Total amount expensed as a period cost

tbs.source: ASC 360-10-30-1 (capitalizable cost of equipment)

tbs.prompt: Delta Co. pays $500,000 cash to acquire a property that consists of land and a building. Independent appraisals value the land at $200,000 and the building at $300,000. Allocate the lump-sum price on a relative-fair-value basis and record the acquisition.

exhibits.title: Relative fair value allocation
exhibits.body: Total appraised value = $500,000. Land = 200,000 / 500,000 = 40%; Building = 300,000 / 500,000 = 60%. Allocate the $500,000 cost in those proportions.

tbs.source: ASC 360-10-30-1 (lump-sum purchase allocated by relative fair value)

tbs.prompt: On January 1, Lessee Co. signs a 4-year FINANCE lease with annual payments of $30,000 due at the END of each year. The rate implicit in the lease is 8%. The right-of-use asset is amortized straight-line over the 4-year term. Determine the amounts below. Round each answer to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: Present value factor
exhibits.body: PV of an ordinary annuity, n=4, i=8% = 3.312127. Initial lease liability = $30,000 x 3.312127 = $99,364 (rounded).

exhibits.title: Year 1 amounts
exhibits.body: Interest = beginning liability x 8%. Principal reduction = $30,000 payment - interest. ROU amortization = initial ROU / 4 years. Ending liability = beginning liability - principal reduction.

tbs.source: ASC 842-20-30-1 and 35 (finance lease measurement and effective-interest amortization)

tbs.prompt: At the end of Year 1, a lessee makes the first $30,000 annual payment on a finance lease whose carrying liability was $99,364 at the start of the year (implicit rate 8%). Split the payment between interest and principal and record it.
tbs.source: ASC 842-20-35 (finance lease subsequent measurement)

tbs.prompt: Nova Co. enters a $200,000 contract with three distinct performance obligations: a software license (standalone selling price $120,000), installation ($40,000), and one year of support ($60,000). Total standalone selling price is $220,000, so the contract discount is allocated on a relative-standalone-selling-price basis. Allocate the $200,000 transaction price. Round to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: Relative SSP allocation
exhibits.body: Allocation = $200,000 x (obligation SSP / $220,000 total SSP). License = 120/220; Installation = 40/220; Support = 60/220. Allocations sum to the $200,000 transaction price.

steps.label: Transaction price allocated to the software license
Transaction price allocated to the installation
Transaction price allocated to the one-year support

tbs.source: ASC 606-10-32-31 through 32-33 (allocation on a relative standalone selling price basis)

tbs.prompt: Summit Builders has a 3-year fixed-price construction contract for $5,000,000 that qualifies for over-time revenue recognition using the cost-to-cost input method. By the end of Year 1, costs incurred total $1,200,000 and total estimated costs are $4,000,000. Determine the amounts below. Round to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: Cost-to-cost method
exhibits.body: Percent complete = costs incurred to date / total estimated costs. Revenue to date = percent complete x $5,000,000 transaction price. Gross profit to date = revenue to date - costs incurred to date.

steps.label: Cumulative revenue recognized at end of Year 1
Gross profit recognized at end of Year 1

tbs.source: ASC 606-10-55-17 through 55-21 (measuring progress using an input method)

tbs.prompt: At year-end, Harbor Co.'s trading securities portfolio has an aggregate cost of $250,000 and a fair value of $268,000. Trading securities are measured at fair value with holding gains and losses recognized in net income. Determine the amounts below.

exhibits.title: Trading measurement
exhibits.body: Unrealized holding gain/loss = fair value - cost, recognized in net income. Balance-sheet carrying amount = fair value.

steps.label: Unrealized holding gain reported in net income
Carrying amount of the portfolio on the balance sheet

tbs.source: ASC 320-10-35-1 (trading securities at fair value through net income)

tbs.prompt: At year-end, Harbor Co.'s trading securities have risen in value from a $250,000 cost basis to a $268,000 fair value. Record the fair value adjustment for the trading portfolio.

exhibits.title: Adjustment
exhibits.body: The $18,000 increase (fair value $268,000 - cost $250,000) is recognized through net income for a trading portfolio.

tbs.source: ASC 320-10-35-1 (trading securities fair value adjustment through earnings)

tbs.prompt: On January 1, Year 1, Lessee Co. enters a 5-year lease for equipment. Annual lease payments of $50,000 are due at the END of each year. The rate implicit in the lease is 6%, which the lessee uses. Lessee Co. also pays $3,000 in initial direct costs in cash at commencement. The lease is classified as a FINANCE lease. Prepare the journal entry to record the lease at commencement (January 1, Year 1). Round the measured lease liability to the nearest dollar.

exhibits.title: Present value factor
exhibits.body: PV of an ordinary annuity, n=5, i=6% = 4.212364. Lease liability = $50,000 x 4.212364 = $210,618 (rounded). ROU asset = lease liability + initial direct costs.

tbs.source: ASC 842-20-30-1 (initial measurement of lease liability and right-of-use asset)

tbs.prompt: During Year 1, Builder Co. self-constructed a warehouse. It paid $800,000 cash for materials and labor directly attributable to construction. Avoidable interest on construction borrowings for the period was $45,000, all paid in cash, and qualifies for capitalization. Builder Co. also paid $12,000 cash for routine repairs to existing equipment used at the site. Prepare the single summary journal entry to record these cash payments, capitalizing what should be capitalized and expensing what should be expensed.

exhibits.title: Cost classification
exhibits.body: Materials/labor ($800,000) and avoidable interest ($45,000) are capitalized to the Building. Routine repairs ($12,000) are period expense. Total cash paid = $857,000.

tbs.source: ASC 835-20-30-1 and ASC 360-10 (capitalization of interest and self-constructed asset cost)

tbs.prompt: On January 1, Year 1, Investor Co. purchases, as a held-to-maturity investment, $500,000 face value 5-year bonds paying 5% stated interest ANNUALLY on December 31. The market (effective) rate at issuance is 6%. Interest is accounted for using the effective-interest method. Compute the requested amounts. Round each answer to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: Year 1 effective-interest computation
exhibits.body: Interest revenue = carrying value x 6%. Discount amortization = interest revenue - cash coupon. Ending carrying value = beginning carrying value + amortization.

steps.label: Purchase price (initial carrying value) of the bonds
Carrying value of the investment at December 31, Year 1

tbs.source: ASC 320-10-35 and ASC 835-30-35-2 (HTM debt securities; effective-interest amortization)

tbs.prompt: Vendor Co. enters a single contract with a customer for a total transaction price of $90,000. The contract contains three distinct performance obligations: equipment, installation, and a 2-year service plan. The standalone selling prices (SSP) are: equipment $60,000, installation $15,000, service $25,000 (total SSP $100,000). Because total SSP exceeds the contract price, the discount is allocated proportionally to all obligations based on relative SSP (Step 4 of the revenue model). Compute the transaction price allocated to each performance obligation. Round to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: Relative SSP allocation
exhibits.body: Allocation = $90,000 x (obligation SSP / $100,000 total SSP). Equipment 60%, Installation 15%, Service 25%. Allocations must sum to the $90,000 transaction price.

steps.label: Transaction price allocated to the equipment
Transaction price allocated to the installation
Transaction price allocated to the 2-year service plan

tbs.source: ASC 606-10-32-31 through 32-33 (allocation of the transaction price based on relative standalone selling prices)

tbs.prompt: On January 1, Year 1, Sierra Co. issues $500,000 face value, 5-year bonds that pay 6% interest annually. Because the market (effective) rate at issuance is 8%, the bonds sell for $460,073. Prepare the journal entry to record the issuance of the bonds.
tbs.source: ASC 835-30-25-2; ASC 470-10 (bonds issued at a discount)

tbs.prompt: At December 31, Year 1, Tango Co. holds available-for-sale (AFS) debt securities with an amortized cost of $120,000; their year-end fair value is $131,000, and no credit loss exists. Prepare the year-end journal entry to adjust the securities to fair value.

exhibits.title: Fair value change
exhibits.body: Fair value $131,000 - amortized cost $120,000 = $11,000 unrealized holding gain. For AFS debt securities the gain is recorded in other comprehensive income, not net income.

tbs.source: ASC 320-10-35-1 (available-for-sale debt securities measured at fair value through OCI)

tbs.prompt: Foxtrot Corp reacquires 2,000 shares of its own $1 par common stock on the open market for $18 per share. The company uses the cost method to account for treasury stock. Prepare the journal entry to record the reacquisition.

exhibits.title: Cost method
exhibits.body: Treasury stock is recorded at total reacquisition cost: 2,000 shares x $18 = $36,000. Par value is ignored under the cost method until the shares are later reissued.

tbs.source: ASC 505-30-30-2 (treasury stock, cost method)

tbs.prompt: For Year 1, Golf Co. reports pretax financial income of $600,000. Its only book-tax difference is $80,000 of tax depreciation (MACRS) in excess of book depreciation, a taxable temporary difference. The enacted tax rate is 21%. Prepare the journal entry to record Year 1 income taxes.

exhibits.title: Tax computation
exhibits.body: Taxable income = $600,000 - $80,000 = $520,000; current tax payable = $520,000 x 21% = $109,200. Total tax expense = $600,000 x 21% = $126,000. Deferred tax liability = $80,000 x 21% = $16,800.

tbs.source: ASC 740-10-25 and 740-10-30 (current and deferred income tax provision)

tbs.prompt: Hotel Co. has 100,000 shares of $1 par common stock outstanding and declares a 10% common stock dividend when the market price is $15 per share. Because the dividend is small (below the 20-25% threshold), it is capitalized at fair value. Prepare the journal entry to record the declaration.

exhibits.title: Small stock dividend
exhibits.body: Shares issued = 100,000 x 10% = 10,000. Fair value capitalized = 10,000 x $15 = $150,000. Common stock dividend distributable = 10,000 x $1 par = $10,000; the $140,000 excess is additional paid-in capital.

tbs.source: ASC 505-20-30-3 (small stock dividend capitalized at fair value)

tbs.prompt: On January 1, Year 1, Igloo Co. issues $200,000 face value, 3-year bonds with a 5% annual stated rate (interest paid each December 31). The market (effective) yield is 7%. Using the effective-interest method, compute the requested amounts. Round each answer to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: Present value factors (i = 7%, n = 3)
exhibits.body: PV of $1 = 0.816298; PV of an ordinary annuity = 2.624316. Annual coupon = $200,000 x 5% = $10,000.

exhibits.title: Effective-interest method
exhibits.body: Issue price = $200,000 x 0.816298 + $10,000 x 2.624316. Year-1 interest expense = carrying value x 7%; discount amortization = interest expense - coupon; ending carrying value = beginning carrying value + amortization.

steps.label: Issue price (initial carrying value) of the bonds
Carrying value of the bonds at December 31, Year 1

tbs.source: ASC 835-30-35-2 (effective-interest amortization of a bond discount)

tbs.prompt: Juliet Co. buys a machine for $100,000 with a 5-year useful life and a $10,000 estimated salvage value, and depreciates it using the double-declining-balance (DDB) method. Compute the requested amounts. Round to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: DDB method
exhibits.body: DDB rate = 2 x (1/5) = 40%, applied to beginning book value each year; salvage value is ignored in the rate (it only serves as a floor). Year 1 = 40% x $100,000; Year 2 = 40% x the remaining book value.

steps.label: Book value at the end of Year 2

tbs.source: ASC 360-10-35-4 (declining-balance depreciation)

tbs.prompt: Kilo Co.'s defined benefit pension plan has a projected benefit obligation (PBO) of $900,000 at the beginning of Year 1. During Year 1: service cost is $70,000, the discount rate is 5%, benefits of $40,000 are paid to retirees, and an actuarial loss of $15,000 increases the PBO. Compute the requested amounts. Round to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: PBO roll-forward
exhibits.body: Ending PBO = beginning PBO + service cost + interest cost - benefits paid +/- actuarial loss or gain. Interest cost = beginning PBO x discount rate.

steps.label: Projected benefit obligation (PBO) at December 31, Year 1

tbs.source: ASC 715-30-35 (components of the projected benefit obligation)

tbs.prompt: Lima Co. reports net income of $500,000 and had 200,000 weighted-average common shares outstanding during the year. It also has 30,000 stock options outstanding with a $10 exercise price; the average market price of the stock during the year was $25. Using the treasury-stock method, compute the requested amounts. Round earnings-per-share amounts to the nearest cent and share amounts to the nearest whole share.

exhibits.title: Treasury-stock method
exhibits.body: Assumed proceeds = 30,000 x $10 = $300,000; shares repurchased at the average price = $300,000 / $25 = 12,000; incremental shares = 30,000 - 12,000. Basic EPS = net income / weighted-average shares; diluted EPS uses that denominator plus the incremental shares.

steps.label: Incremental shares added under the treasury-stock method

tbs.source: ASC 260-10-45 (basic and diluted EPS; treasury-stock method)

tbs.prompt: On January 1, Year 1, Mike Co. acquires 30% of the voting common stock of November Inc. for $600,000 and accounts for the investment under the equity method. During Year 1, November reports net income of $200,000 and pays total cash dividends of $50,000. Compute the requested amounts. Round to the nearest whole dollar.

exhibits.title: Equity method
exhibits.body: The investor records its share of investee net income as income and increases the investment; dividends received reduce the investment carrying amount (they are not income). Ending investment = cost + share of net income - share of dividends.

steps.label: Mike's equity in November's Year-1 earnings
Dividends received from November during Year 1
Carrying amount of the Investment in November at December 31, Year 1

tbs.source: ASC 323-10-35 (equity method of accounting for investments)

section_items.prompt: The CPA firm Adams, Barnes & Company is preparing for the Year 20X5 audit of Keystone Computers & Networks, Inc. (KCN), a calendar year-end nonissuer. A staff member updated last year's audit request list. As senior, review each called-out request and decide whether to retain it, delete it, or replace it with a better request, using the exhibits. Materiality is $300,000.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1 - KCN working trial balance (condensed)
exhibits.body: Materiality for the Year 20X5 audit = $300,000. Selected balances (12/31/X4 audited vs 12/31/X5 unadjusted): Accounts receivable-trade (1050.10); Software development cost (2100.00, newly capitalized this year); Leasehold improvements; Capital stock (unchanged X4 to X5); Rent (7100.10, significant); Utilities (7140.10, immaterial).

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2 - Memo to files (partner C. Adams, 11/5/20X5)
exhibits.body: The Plumbtree intangible (~$1,000,000 remaining, amortized $200k/yr) may be written off half or more this year; earnings are down ~40%. The company began capitalizing software development costs this year. Accounts receivable from officers increased because President Best asked for and obtained a loan from the company under a loan agreement Mr. Steele signed; because the amount was slightly below the level needing Board approval, the matter was not addressed by the Board.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3 - Forwarded email (President Best)
exhibits.body: "...let's make sure that we capitalize these costs ($178,000) because we may be able to develop software..." PS: rent is going up, but the new lease has no payment for the first three months (abatement).

exhibits.title: Exhibit 4 - Email (Manager Karen West)
exhibits.body: Allowance for bad debts $96,000 -> $104,000; Willay Co. receivable of $208,234 written off; ~$32,000 more possibly uncollectible. We definitely need to consider the adequacy of the allowance for bad debts.

exhibits.title: Keystone Computers & Networks, Inc. - Year Ended December 31, 20X5 - Audit Request List (Draft 1)
exhibits.body:
For each called-out request, determine if the current language is appropriate as is, should be removed, or should be replaced with one of the provided alternatives (materiality = $300,000).
1. <blank step="s1">Accounts receivable summarized by date of most recent purchase.</blank>
2. <blank step="s2">Invoices supporting depreciation expense for the year.</blank>
3. <blank step="s3">Software development cost invoices and other support.</blank>
4. <blank step="s4">Vouchers supporting increase in leasehold improvements.</blank>
5. <blank step="s5">Detailed analysis and reconciliation of each unpaid account payable at year-end.</blank>
6. <blank step="s6">Documentation supporting change in capital stock outstanding.</blank>
7. <blank step="s7">Management's review of receivables from officers.</blank>
8. <blank step="s8">Account 7140.10 expense analysis.</blank>

steps.label: Callout 1 - A/R summary basis
steps.original_text: Accounts receivable summarized by date of most recent purchase.

options.text: Accounts receivable aged by date due.
Accounts receivable summarized by length of business relationship with KCN.
Accounts receivable matched with sales items.

steps.label: Callout 2 - depreciation support
steps.original_text: Invoices supporting depreciation expense for the year.

options.text: Lawyer's letter response on expected life of fixed assets.
Cash deposit slip for increase in cash for 20X5.
Schedule of details of depreciation expense calculation for 20X5.

steps.label: Callout 3 - software development costs
steps.original_text: Software development cost invoices and other support.

section_items.prompt: Review the draft revenue-recognition footnote (Note 7) for a SaaS company reporting under ASC 606. For each highlighted phrase, retain it or replace it so the note is correct, using the exhibits.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1 - Standard contract terms
exhibits.body: Annual SaaS subscriptions are billed in advance; the service is delivered continuously over the 12-month term. Customers have a 30-day right of return. A perpetual license and one year of post-contract support (PCS) are separate performance obligations, each with an observable standalone selling price.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3 - Email from controller
exhibits.body: Returns are estimable from history; they occur every year and are not zero, though individually small. Please make sure the note reflects the returns policy.

exhibits.title: Note 7. Revenue Recognition (draft)
exhibits.body: The Company recognizes revenue under ASC 606. For its annual SaaS subscription contracts, the Company recognizes subscription revenue <blank step="s1">at the point the contract is signed</blank>. Implementation services that are not distinct from the subscription are recognized <blank step="s2">over the subscription term</blank>. The Company's standard terms include a 30-day right of return; the Company records revenue <blank step="s3">in full at contract inception with no adjustment for expected returns</blank>. For bundled arrangements that include a perpetual software license and one year of technical support, the transaction price is allocated <blank step="s4">entirely to the software license</blank>. Total subscription revenue recognized in 20X5 was <blank step="s5">$4,200,000</blank>.

section_items.prompt: A sole proprietor asks whether the ordinary and necessary costs of carrying on the business are deductible. Research the Internal Revenue Code for the section allowing a deduction for trade or business expenses, and enter the citation.

exhibits.title: Scenario
exhibits.body: Identify the IRC section that allows a current deduction for ordinary and necessary trade or business expenses.

section_items.source: IRC §162(a) (trade or business expenses). 26 U.S.C. §162(a) is public domain (verbatim in corpus). VERIFIED current. Contrast §263(a) (capitalization).

section_items.prompt: Review the draft tax memo. For each highlighted expenditure, select whether it is currently deductible under IRC §162 or must be capitalized and recovered over time under IRC §263. Use the exhibits.

exhibits.body: Client (a calendar-year business) incurred: a routine repair to a delivery truck ($400); the purchase of a new $60,000 machine placed in service this year; the cost to add a new wing to the building ($250,000); and office supplies used this year ($900).

exhibits.title: Draft tax memo - treatment of Year 1 expenditures
exhibits.body: Recommended treatment: the routine repair to the delivery truck is <blank step="b1">[treatment]</blank>. The purchase of the new machine is <blank step="b2">[treatment]</blank>. The cost to add the new building wing is <blank step="b3">[treatment]</blank>. The office supplies used this year are <blank step="b4">[treatment]</blank>.

section_items.source: REG tax-memo doc-review (deduct vs capitalize). Authority: IRC §162 (ordinary/necessary business expenses currently deductible) vs §263(a) (new assets/permanent improvements capitalized). VERIFIED current; IRC public domain (verbatim in corpus).
